caveats: a: Occupation shares are not task shares and are not wage weighted.
a: The six-digit occupation source is a commercial secondary summary, while BLS publishes the detailed official matrix only at broader NAICS 237100.
a: Safety-critical judgment, site variability, and regulatory records require accountable human review even where drafting is automated.
rho: No source measures five-year AI implementation for 237120.
rho: Customer data-access restrictions and critical-infrastructure cybersecurity can materially slow deployment.
rho: The estimate excludes pricing, demand, and valuation effects.
e: The Census definition establishes included activities but not the share fitting the narrowed lens.
e: The injected firm count is margin-bridged and may include firms outside the normalized EBITDA band or with mixed NAICS activities.
e: A nominal MSA or preferred-vendor relationship does not guarantee recurring revenue or transferable customer approval.
s5: Census age data are economy-wide, data year 2018, and describe responding owners rather than target-band firms.
s5: BizBuySell transactions include construction subsectors outside 237120 and typically have earnings below the target EBITDA band.
s5: There is no eligible-firm denominator, so the observed transaction count is not a transfer rate.
q: A single diversified public-company filing is not representative of the complete LMM contract mix.
q: Commodity cycles and customer capital budgets can weaken contractor bargaining power at renewal.
q: The range excludes implementation difficulty and demand-volume effects.
d5: Pipeline capacity and mileage are not contractor output and do not capture project complexity or real prices.
d5: The natural-gas outlook is stronger than the oil-pipeline outlook, and the mix of the narrowed population is unknown.
d5: Regulatory and safety rules can create replacement work but can also delay or prevent new projects.
d5: LNG-terminal construction itself is largely outside the narrowed lens; the relevant effect is connecting pipeline and station demand.
o: This estimates operator requirement, not labor intensity or headcount.
o: Utilities and midstream operators may internalize some work, and large primes may absorb specialty scope.
o: Trenchless, robotic, and prefabricated methods can alter crew hours without removing contractor accountability.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project, work-order, and master-service-agreement revenue from gas utilities, pipeline and midstream operators, producers, industrial customers, and large primes, commonly under fixed-price, unit-price, or schedule-of-rates structures. Repeat revenue arises from successive expansions, replacements, integrity programs, and maintenance capital rather than subscriptions.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS code combines repeat line-construction and integrity contractors with refinery, petrochemical, storage, and process-facility construction. The lens is narrowed to pipeline and directly related station/tank field work because large process-plant EPC has a different delivery model, capital scale, engineering intensity, and transfer market that would make one screen incoherent.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive utility or midstream crews, shells, financing vehicles, one-project entities, visual-only inspection classified elsewhere, oil-well rig work classified elsewhere, and refinery, petrochemical-plant, or large process-facility EPC whose scale, engineering content, risk allocation, and project cadence are operationally different from repeat pipeline field services.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market external contractors repeatedly performing oil and natural-gas gathering, transmission, distribution, and service-line construction, replacement, rehabilitation, integrity digs, corrosion work, and directly related pumping, compression, metering, and storage-tank field work.

a.bridge: The sources measure occupation shares, not wage-weighted task-level AI exposure or current automation. The exact-industry secondary summary reports 55% construction/extraction, 16% installation/maintenance, 7% office support, and 6% management, while the official BLS broader 237100 matrix supplies detail for estimating and project-management roles. I map estimating, scheduling, document control, compliance, cost tracking, procurement, billing, and portions of supervision to exposed tasks, with little substitution assigned to welding, excavation, equipment operation, inspection execution, and repair. Broader 237100 includes power/communications and water work, so it may misstate the target mix in either direction; the secondary six-digit summary may inherit unpublished or older BLS estimates.
caveats: Occupation shares are not task shares and are not wage weighted.
The six-digit occupation source is a commercial secondary summary, while BLS publishes the detailed official matrix only at broader NAICS 237100.
Safety-critical judgment, site variability, and regulatory records require accountable human review even where drafting is automated.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census for 237120 contractors using payroll job codes and time studies, separated into pipeline newbuild, distribution replacement, integrity/rehabilitation, station/tank work, and excluded process-plant EPC, with measured current automation penetration.
a.rationale: The work is predominantly physical and field-based: the six-digit summary reports construction laborers at 21% and equipment operators at 10%, in addition to substantial installation and repair employment. AI opportunity is concentrated in bid and work-package preparation, plans/specification review, scheduling, daily reports, integrity-record retrieval, compliance packages, change documentation, invoice reconciliation, and management coordination rather than autonomous pipe construction.

d5.bridge: PHMSA mileage and replacement data, EIA capacity additions and energy outlooks, and a contractor filing measure installed infrastructure, capacity, energy-market demand, and customer spending drivers rather than a five-year constant-price, constant-quality quantity index for the narrowed contractor basket. I translate continuing gas/LNG expansion plus integrity and replacement work into modest base-case growth, while applying a wide downside for oil-price cycles, permitting, project deferrals, transition policy, owner self-performance, and completion of the current LNG build wave. Capacity additions can overstate sustained construction quantity; installed mileage understates replacement and station work.
caveats: Pipeline capacity and mileage are not contractor output and do not capture project complexity or real prices.
The natural-gas outlook is stronger than the oil-pipeline outlook, and the mix of the narrowed population is unknown.
Regulatory and safety rules can create replacement work but can also delay or prevent new projects.
LNG-terminal construction itself is largely outside the narrowed lens; the relevant effect is connecting pipeline and station demand.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-dollar 2026-2031 forecast of addressable contractor work using EIA and FERC project trackers, PHMSA replacement mileage, state utility rate cases, operator capital plans and 10-Ks, integrity-management schedules, Dodge or ConstructConnect starts, and LMM contractor backlog by service line.
d5.rationale: Gas-linked work has visible support: EIA reports 2025 pipeline completions added about 6.3 Bcf/d, 85% directed to the South Central region, and projects LNG export capacity to reach 27.7 Bcf/d by 2030. PHMSA reports 413,330 miles of gas transmission and gathering pipe in 2025 and an ongoing policy focus on repair, rehabilitation, and replacement of high-risk pipe. The base case is only moderate because oil/gas capital spending is cyclical, newbuild projects are lumpy and permitting-sensitive, and some replacement programs are already well advanced.

caveats: The Census definition establishes included activities but not the share fitting the narrowed lens.
The injected firm count is margin-bridged and may include firms outside the normalized EBITDA band or with mixed NAICS activities.
A nominal MSA or preferred-vendor relationship does not guarantee recurring revenue or transferable customer approval.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the injected LMM population using service mix, customer concentration, MSA status, repeat-customer revenue, geography and basin exposure, operator qualifications, safety record, bonding, license continuity, owner dependence, and share of excluded plant-EPC work.
e.rationale: The narrowed population includes external contractors with repeat work under utility, midstream, producer, and prime-contractor programs. Eligibility is reduced because the NAICS code also contains materially different refinery and petrochemical construction, and because some LMM firms are captive, effectively captive to one customer or basin, one-project vehicles, highly owner-dependent, insufficiently qualified or bonded, or lack transferable customer and safety credentials.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 237120 ($14,500,752,449) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 237120 ($49,610,752,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0796 (margins.json[23]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Engineering/Construction EBITDA/Sales 7.96%); revenue/employee $282,072 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 237120); class <5: 549 firms, avg revenue/firm $774,033 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $61,613 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 238 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,959,534 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $155,979 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 247 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,061,834 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $323,322 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 467 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,013,773 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $956,296 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 178 firms, avg revenue/firm $52,017,118 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,140,563 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 86 firms, avg revenue/firm $381,938,221 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $30,402,282 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 178

caveats: This estimates operator requirement, not labor intensity or headcount.
Utilities and midstream operators may internalize some work, and large primes may absorb specialty scope.
Trenchless, robotic, and prefabricated methods can alter crew hours without removing contractor accountability.
o.change_evidence: Project-level evidence on autonomous trenching, welding, coating, inspection and repair; utility or operator self-performance; modularization; and software-only displacement, measured as eliminated external contractor quantity rather than reduced administrative labor.
o.rationale: Nearly all surviving year-five quantity requires an accountable contractor to mobilize qualified crews and equipment, excavate or bore, weld or join pipe, manage live-system interfaces, construct stations, test and commission assets, meet environmental and safety requirements, document traceability, and warrant work. Software can improve planning, inspection targeting, records, and coordination but cannot eliminate accountable physical execution for the current service basket.

q.bridge: Primoris is a large diversified infrastructure contractor rather than an LMM pure-play 237120 firm, but its SEC filing directly describes substantial fixed-price and unit-price revenue and pipeline demand. I infer that savings on awarded fixed/unit-price work are initially retained, then reduce retention for transparent unit rates, MSA repricing, competitive rebids, customer productivity demands, shared savings, and claims negotiation. Large-contractor risk allocation and bargaining power may differ from LMM firms in either direction; fixed-price prevalence can upward-bias retention if cost overruns or customers renegotiate scope.
caveats: A single diversified public-company filing is not representative of the complete LMM contract mix.
Commodity cycles and customer capital budgets can weaken contractor bargaining power at renewal.
The range excludes implementation difficulty and demand-volume effects.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level comparison of realized gross benefits, unit rates, bid spreads, MSA renewals, change-order recovery, customer concessions, and margin persistence for LMM pipeline contractors across utility distribution, integrity, gathering, transmission, and station work.
q.rationale: Fixed-price and unit-price contracts give contractors an initial claim on cost savings, particularly where AI reduces bid effort, missed documentation, schedule leakage, invoice delay, and unrecovered changes. Over five years, sophisticated utilities and midstream clients can benchmark unit rates and capture part of visible productivity through rebids and MSA renewals. Benefits tied to throughput, risk avoidance, and evidence quality may be retained more durably than direct labor reductions.

caveats: No source measures five-year AI implementation for 237120.
Customer data-access restrictions and critical-infrastructure cybersecurity can materially slow deployment.
The estimate excludes pricing, demand, and valuation effects.
rho.change_evidence: Twelve-month before/after labor-hour, cycle-time, rework, claim-recovery, audit-finding, and safety-quality results from at least 12 LMM pipeline contractors deploying AI across estimating, project controls, integrity documentation, field reporting, and finance.
rho.rationale: About half of exposed opportunity could plausibly be implemented within five years through assisted estimating, work planning, document intake, schedule/cost variance detection, daily-report drafting, integrity-record retrieval, invoice support, and knowledge search. Adoption is constrained by fragmented field data, customer-controlled systems, cybersecurity requirements, PHMSA recordkeeping, environmental and safety obligations, union and qualification rules, legacy estimating/accounting integration, and the severe consequence of incorrect technical or compliance output.

s5.bridge: Census measures age for responding owners of employer firms economy-wide, and BizBuySell measures reported sales in broad building/construction categories, not five-year control transfers among eligible 237120 LMM contractors. I use the 51% age-55-plus owner share as succession pressure and 3,142 reported construction sales during 2021-2025 as evidence of a functioning market, then discount for continued ownership, internal succession, closure, strategic tuck-ins outside the marketplace, and transaction failure from safety, bonding, qualification, customer-consent, or cyclicality issues. Age can overstate near-term sales; marketplace data can undercount private transfers but overrepresent smaller, simpler firms.
caveats: Census age data are economy-wide, data year 2018, and describe responding owners rather than target-band firms.
BizBuySell transactions include construction subsectors outside 237120 and typically have earnings below the target EBITDA band.
There is no eligible-firm denominator, so the observed transaction count is not a transfer rate.
s5.change_evidence: 237120-specific owner-age and succession-plan data matched with five years of closed control transactions from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, SBA change-of-ownership loans, pipeline-focused brokers, sureties, and customer prequalification records.
s5.rationale: Owner aging and observed construction-business sales support a material but minority five-year transfer probability. Pipeline contractors face additional transfer friction because customer qualification, safety statistics, skilled crews, bonding, equipment, working capital, and key management relationships must survive a control change, while commodity and project cycles can defer sale timing.

principal_driver: Gas/LNG connection demand and mandatory integrity, rehabilitation, and replacement work support repeat physical contractor activity while document-heavy workflows offer practical AI leverage.
principal_weakness: The addressable population is small and heterogeneous, demand is project- and commodity-cycle dependent, and most labor remains safety-critical field execution that AI cannot substitute.

run_meta.title: Oil and Gas Pipeline and Related Structures Construction

sources.supports: The reachable six-digit industry page reports construction/extraction occupations at 55% of workforce, installation/maintenance at 16%, office support at 7%, management at 6%, construction laborers at 21%, and construction equipment operators at 10%.
sources.title: Oil and Gas Pipeline and Related Structures Construction - Industry Statistics

sources.supports: Official BLS broader-industry matrix reports construction/extraction at 54.4%, office support 6.4%, management 7.4%, business and financial operations 4.8%, construction managers 3.6%, project management specialists 2.3%, and cost estimators 0.8% of 2024 employment.
sources.title: National Employment Matrix: Utility system construction (237100), 2024-2034

sources.supports: Explains that integrity-management performance tracks assessed pipeline mileage and repairs in and outside high-consequence areas, and that gas-distribution performance tracks leaks eliminated and repaired, supporting the recurring safety and integrity workload and its regulated documentation.
sources.title: PHMSA National Pipeline Performance Measures

sources.supports: States that a substantial portion of revenue comes from fixed-price and unit-price contracts; pipeline demand depends on midstream and petrochemical operating and capital spending; pipeline safety legislation may increase maintenance, integrity, and repair demand; and actual costs can differ from estimates and reduce profit or cause losses.
sources.title: Primoris Services Corporation 2025 Form 10-K

sources.supports: Defines the code to include oil and gas lines, mains, refineries, storage tanks, new work, reconstruction, rehabilitation, repairs, gathering and distribution lines, pumping stations, natural-gas processing plants, and petrochemical plants, supporting the heterogeneity judgment and activity boundaries.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition: 237120 Oil and Gas Pipeline and Related Structures Construction

sources.supports: Census reports 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older, 43% were 35-54, and 6% were 34 or younger, based on the 2019 Annual Business Survey for data year 2018.
sources.title: Business Owners' Ages: Over Half of U.S. Business Owners Were Age 55 and Over

sources.supports: Reports 3,142 sold building and construction businesses analyzed for 2021-2025, with median revenue $1,509,665, median owner earnings $323,174, and median days on market 207, demonstrating a broad construction transfer market but not a 237120 rate.
sources.title: Construction Business Valuation Multiples and Financial Benchmarks

sources.supports: Reports 413,330 total gas transmission and gathering miles in 2025, comprising 300,161 transmission miles and 113,170 gathering miles, versus 411,847 total miles in 2024.
sources.title: PHMSA Annual Report Mileage for Natural Gas Transmission and Gathering Systems

sources.supports: Describes the federal call to accelerate repair, rehabilitation, and replacement of highest-risk pipeline infrastructure; notes many cast and wrought iron pipelines were installed over 60 years ago; and reports about 1% of gas distribution pipe remained iron at year-end 2025.
sources.title: PHMSA Pipeline Replacement Background

sources.supports: EIA reports U.S. natural-gas pipeline projects completed in 2025 added about 6.3 Bcf/d of capacity, with 85% or 5.3 Bcf/d directed to the South Central region and 65% of completed capacity intrastate.
sources.title: Most natural gas pipelines built in 2025 connect the South Central United States to supply

sources.supports: Projects U.S. LNG export capacity to increase to 27.7 Bcf/d by 2030 from 14.9 Bcf/d of LNG exports in 2025, projects pipeline exports to Canada and Mexico to reach about 12.6 Bcf/d in most cases, and describes growing natural-gas demand across almost all cases.
sources.title: EIA Annual Energy Outlook 2026 Narrative

title: Oil and Gas Pipeline and Related Structures Construction
